sections.heading: What is REST?
sections.body: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications. It uses standard HTTP methods and status codes to create a consistent interface for clients to interact with resources.

sections.heading: RESTful Routing Principles
sections.body: REST routes are organized around resources (nouns), not actions. Each resource has a unique URL, and you use HTTP methods to perform actions on them:

code.snippet:
GET    /api/users      - Get all users
GET    /api/users/123  - Get user with ID 123
POST   /api/users      - Create a new user
PUT    /api/users/123  - Update user 123
DELETE /api/users/123  - Delete user 123
code.explanation: Notice how the same URL (/api/users) is used with different HTTP methods to perform different operations.

sections.heading: HTTP Methods and Status Codes
sections.body: Each HTTP method has a specific purpose: GET reads data, POST creates data, PUT updates data, DELETE removes data. Status codes tell the client what happened: 200 OK, 201 Created, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error.

code.explanation: These routes follow REST conventions: GET returns data, POST creates and returns 201 status.

jargon.definition: Representational State Transfer - an architectural style for designing web APIs using standard HTTP methods.
An entity that can be accessed and manipulated via the API, like users, products, or orders.
A specific URL where an API can be accessed, combined with an HTTP method.

summary: REST uses standard HTTP methods and URLs to create predictable, scalable APIs. Resources are nouns, HTTP methods are verbs. Always use appropriate status codes to indicate success or failure.
